claim: The zodiac and astronomical sections use a label-register encoding â€” short fixed-length labels drawn from a closed vocabulary â€” rather than running prose, which would produce anomalously low entropy (observed: zodiac 3.7149 bits, astronomical 3.7471 bits) and a high type-token ratio relative to sections with running text.
evidence_for: Zodiac section has lowest entropy of all sections (3.7149 bits) consistent with a repetitive label vocabulary
Label-heavy sections would reuse a small closed set of words (month names, star names, degree markers) inflating token repetition
Belief [0.337] at moderate confidence already supports this interpretation for zodiac/astronomical sections
evidence_against: Low entropy could also reflect heavier use of homophones in these sections rather than label structure
If labels, the type-token ratio should be distinctly lower than herbal/recipes sections â€” this must be confirmed, not assumed
Pharmaceutical section also has low entropy (3.7772 bits) but is not obviously label-oriented
test: Compute type-token ratio (TTR = unique_words / total_words) and vocabulary coverage at top-50 most frequent words for each section. Compare zodiac and astronomical TTR against herbal, recipes, and text-only sections using a Fisher exact test on vocabulary overlap.
test_metric: TTR per section; percentage of total tokens covered by top-50 word types per section
test_threshold: Zodiac/astronomical TTR < 0.25 AND top-50 word coverage > 60% of tokens would confirm label-register structure; TTR > 0.40 or top-50 coverage < 40% would refute it

reasoning: Label registers and running prose have markedly different TTR profiles; this test requires no decipherment and directly confirms or eliminates the structural explanation for the entropy anomaly in the smallest sections
